title: String literals are &str
instruction:
String literals are `&str` — references to text embedded in the binary. Immutable and fixed-size.

Run this.
successMessage: &str is a view into text. No heap allocation. Fast and lightweight.

title: Owned String
instruction:
`String::from()` creates a heap-allocated string you can grow and modify.

Run this.
successMessage: String is growable. push_str appends a &str to it. Must be declared mut.

title: push a character
instruction:
`push` appends a single `char`. `push_str` appends a string slice.

Run this.
successMessage: push and push_str are the two ways to extend a String. push takes a char (single quotes), push_str takes a &str (double quotes).

title: format! macro
instruction:
`format!` creates a new String from parts without printing.

```rust
let s = format!("{} {}", first, second);
```

Run this.
successMessage: format! is the cleanest way to build a String from multiple pieces. It does not take ownership of its arguments.

title: Common string methods
instruction:
Strings have many built-in methods.

Run this.
successMessage: trim removes whitespace from both ends. to_uppercase, contains, replace — these methods do not modify the original, they return new values.

title: Splitting strings
instruction:
`split()` returns an iterator of string slices. Collect it into a Vec.

Run this.
successMessage: split(',') produces an iterator, collect() turns it into a Vec. This pattern — iterate then collect — appears everywhere in Rust.

title: starts_with and ends_with
instruction:
Check the beginning or end of a string:

Run this.
successMessage: find returns Option<usize> — Some(index) if found, None if not. You will learn Option fully in the enums chapter.

title: Convert to String and back
instruction:
`.to_string()` converts anything printable to a String. `.parse()` goes the other way.

Run this.
successMessage: to_string and parse are the bridge between text and numbers. unwrap assumes success — you will learn safer error handling soon.
